subject: Re: Differences between Windows Warp and WarpTools motion correction
body:
Hi James,

Just a little caution here: if it's hampering the alignment of your tilt
series, it's not just a cosmetic issue. This can potentially prevent you
from reconstructing good tomograms and finding particles in them. Sometimes
ccderaser is the difference between success and complete failure in
tomogram reconstruction. Consider running it as suggested by Alister.

Best wishes,

--
Ricardo Diogo Righetto


Em qua., 29 de jan. de 2025 às 16:08, James Zhen <jzhen97@ucla.edu>
escreveu:

> Thank you, Alister! I will give ccderaser a try.
>
> It's also comforting to know that it's primarily a cosmetic issue.
>
> Best,
> James
>
>
>
> On Wed, Jan 29, 2025 at 12:29 AM Reika Watanabe <
> reika.watanabe.c@gmail.com> wrote:
>
>> Thank you very much for your kind attention and insight.
>> Reika
>>
>> On Tue, Jan 28, 2025, 15:23 Alister Burt <alisterburt@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>>> Hi Reika,
>>>
>>> Unlikely!
>>>
>>> Cheers,
>>>
>>> Alister
>>>
>>> On Tue, 28 Jan 2025 at 14:20, Reika Watanabe <reika.watanabe.c@gmail.com>
>>> wrote:
>>>
>>>> Hi Alister,
>>>> A quick question! Do you think the lack of hot pixel removal in
>>>> WarpTool will eventually affect the subtomogram average outcome?
>>>> Thanks for your insight!
>>>> Reika
>>>>
>>>> On Jan 28, 2025, at 11:45 AM, Alister Burt <alisterburt@gmail.com>
>>>> wrote:
>>>>
>>>> Hi James,
>>>>
>>>> Sorry you're running into issues - I suspect these come from hot pixels
>>>> in your data.
>>>> Warp used to remove hot pixels but Dimitry removed that feature citing
>>>> that it was hard to make it work robustly with counting data.
>>>>
>>>> You should be able to avoid issues moving forwards by running ccderaser
>>>> <https://bio3d.colorado.edu/imod/doc/man/ccderaser.html> on your
>>>> motion corrected averages. I will add a note to the worker logs that are
>>>> output :-)
>>>>
>>>> Cheers,
>>>>
>>>> Alister
>>>>
>>>>
>>>> On Tue, 28 Jan 2025 at 07:25, James Zhen <jzhen97@g.ucla.edu> wrote:
>>>>
>>>>> Hello all,
>>>>>
>>>>> I am working on a FIB dataset and noticed that WarpTools 2.0.0dev31
>>>>> motion correction produced averages with washed out contrast and reduced
>>>>> dynamic range, especially for the high tilts. Aretomo alignment of these
>>>>> stacks has shifts greater than 2000 pixels for the washed out high tilts,
>>>>> which causes streaking in center of the tomograms.
>>>>>
>>>>> When processed in Warp 1.0.9 with the same equivalent settings, the
>>>>> dynamic range of the motion corrected averages is better. Aretomo alignment
>>>>> also proceeds normally with shifts in the expected range for all tilts, and
>>>>> the reconstructions do not have the streaking.
>>>>>
>>>>> <warp-warptools_motion.jpeg>
>>>>> Is there a difference in how motion correction is handled between Warp
>>>>> 1.0.9 and WarpTools 2.0.0dev31?
>>>>>
>>>>> --
